content:
𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗝𝘂𝗱𝗴𝗲 𝗪𝗵𝗼 𝗞𝗻𝗲𝘄 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗣𝗿𝗶𝗰𝗲 𝗼𝗳 𝗛𝗶𝘀 𝗗𝗶𝘀𝘀𝗲𝗻𝘁
Some judgments decide disputes.

Some judgments define the conscience of a nation.

Justice H.R. Khanna’s dissent in 𝗔𝗗𝗠 𝗝𝗮𝗯𝗮𝗹𝗽𝘂𝗿 𝘃 𝗦𝗵𝗶𝘃𝗸𝗮𝗻𝘁 𝗦𝗵𝘂𝗸𝗹𝗮, 𝗔𝗜𝗥 𝟭𝟵𝟳𝟲 𝗦𝗖 𝟭𝟮𝟬𝟳; (1976) 2 SCC 521, popularly known as the 𝗛𝗮𝗯𝗲𝗮𝘀 𝗖𝗼𝗿𝗽𝘂𝘀 𝗖𝗮𝘀𝗲, belongs to that rare second category.

During the Emergency, the Supreme Court was faced with one of the most frightening constitutional questions in Indian history: if the State unlawfully detains a citizen, can that citizen still approach a court for protection of life and personal liberty?

The majority said, in effect, no.

Justice H.R. Khanna stood alone.

His dissent was not merely a legal opinion. It was a constitutional warning. He held that 𝗹𝗶𝗳𝗲 𝗮𝗻𝗱 𝗽𝗲𝗿𝘀𝗼𝗻𝗮𝗹 𝗹𝗶𝗯𝗲𝗿𝘁𝘆 𝗮𝗿𝗲 𝗻𝗼𝘁 𝗴𝗶𝗳𝘁𝘀 𝗴𝗶𝘃𝗲𝗻 𝗯𝘆 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗦𝘁𝗮𝘁𝗲. Even when the enforcement of Article 21 is suspended, the State cannot take away life or liberty without authority of law.

For Justice Khanna, the rule of law was not a luxury of peaceful times.

It was the minimum condition of a constitutional republic.

Before delivering this dissent, Justice Khanna reportedly told his sister:

“𝗜 𝗵𝗮𝘃𝗲 𝗽𝗿𝗲𝗽𝗮𝗿𝗲𝗱 𝗮 𝗷𝘂𝗱𝗴𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝘄𝗵𝗶𝗰𝗵 𝗶𝘀 𝗴𝗼𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝘁𝗼 𝗰𝗼𝘀𝘁 𝗺𝗲 𝘁𝗵𝗲 𝗖𝗵𝗶𝗲𝗳 𝗝𝘂𝘀𝘁𝗶𝗰𝗲𝘀𝗵𝗶𝗽 𝗼𝗳 𝗜𝗻𝗱𝗶𝗮.”

He knew the price.

And he still wrote it.

When the office of Chief Justice of India later fell vacant, Justice Khanna was the senior-most judge. By convention, he should have become the Chief Justice of India. But he was superseded, and Justice M.H. Beg was appointed Chief Justice.

Justice Khanna resigned.

He lost the office, but he preserved the dignity of the Constitution.

That episode had already shown that constitutional courage could carry institutional consequences.

Justice Khanna knew this history.

Yet, when his own moment came, he chose principle over promotion.

Later, Indian constitutional law slowly returned to the path he had defended. In 𝗠𝗮𝗻𝗲𝗸𝗮 𝗚𝗮𝗻𝗱𝗵𝗶 𝘃 𝗨𝗻𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗼𝗳 𝗜𝗻𝗱𝗶𝗮, (𝟭𝟵𝟳𝟴) 𝟭 𝗦𝗖𝗖 𝟮𝟰𝟴, the Supreme Court expanded the meaning of Article 21 and held that any procedure depriving a person of life or personal liberty must be just, fair and reasonable.

The narrow view of liberty began to collapse.

Then came the 44th Constitutional Amendment, which ensured that Articles 20 and 21 could not be suspended in the same manner even during an Emergency.

Finally, in 𝗝𝘂𝘀𝘁𝗶𝗰𝗲 𝗞.𝗦. 𝗣𝘂𝘁𝘁𝗮𝘀𝘄𝗮𝗺𝘆 𝘃 𝗨𝗻𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗼𝗳 𝗜𝗻𝗱𝗶𝗮, (𝟮𝟬𝟭𝟳) 𝟭𝟬 𝗦𝗖𝗖 𝟭, the Supreme Court expressly recognised the correctness of Justice Khanna’s view and overruled the majority decision in 𝗔𝗗𝗠 𝗝𝗮𝗯𝗮𝗹𝗽𝘂𝗿.

History corrected the law.

But Justice Khanna had corrected his conscience much earlier.
He did not become the Chief Justice of India.

But he became something far greater.

------Dr Neel Mani Tripathi
comment: Khanna’s dissent also highlights incentive problem: when career costs follow judicial courage future dissents may shrink unless safeguards limit executive influence appointments.
